# Algal Biomass Conversion

![An abstract depiction symbolizing the shift towards sustainable energy solutions. Two vortex-like forms are shown, one a deep bronze and the other a vibrant yellow, both enclosed in a glowing blue aura, set against a speckled navy backdrop. This visual representation could illustrate the transformation of energy systems, moving from conventional resources towards clean energy technologies and improved energy storage capabilities.](https://energy.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/visual-metaphor-of-energy-transition-depicting-sustainable-power-generation-and.jpg "Visual Metaphor of Energy Transition Depicting Sustainable Power Generation and Environmental Impact")

## Fundamentals

Algal Biomass Conversion, in its simplest, is the process of transforming algae into usable forms of energy. Think of algae as tiny plants that, just like larger plants, use sunlight to grow. They take in carbon dioxide (CO2) and, through photosynthesis, create sugars and oils within their cells. These sugars and oils are a form of stored energy.

The beauty of algae lies in its rapid growth rate and its ability to thrive in various environments, even those unsuitable for traditional agriculture. This opens up possibilities for creating sustainable energy sources without competing with food production.

**Why Convert Algae?** Algae itself, in its raw form, isn’t directly usable in most energy applications. We can’t simply plug algae into our cars or power plants. This is where conversion comes in. Conversion techniques break down the algal biomass (the organic matter that makes up algae) and transform it into more practical energy carriers.

![A striking composition visualizes energy sustainability through abstract forms and materials. Bronze ring element may represent energy transmission and distribution networks, intertwined with cube-like structures indicating energy storage solutions and grid optimization. The cylindrical component symbolizes responsible resource management and lifecycle assessment for sustainable technology.](https://energy.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/sustainable-energy-systems-and-efficient-resource-management-in-industrial.jpg "Sustainable Energy Systems and Efficient Resource Management in Industrial Materials Design")

### Common Conversion Methods

Several methods exist to convert algal biomass, each with its own advantages and disadvantages. These methods can be broadly categorized into biochemical and thermochemical processes.

![An abstract digital artwork embodies the essence of sustainable energy transitions and circular economy principles, visualized through geometric forms and textured surfaces reminiscent of aged industrial materials. Dominated by shades of blue and beige against a dark field, the composition features a central circular element akin to a technological lens, suggesting advanced energy monitoring and smart grids. This representation highlights infrastructure resilience and the integration of digital technology for resource management within energy systems.](https://energy.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/abstract-representation-of-sustainable-energy-infrastructure-and-resource.jpg "Abstract Representation of Sustainable Energy Infrastructure and Resource Management for a Circular Economy")

### What Do We Get out of It?

Algal Biomass Conversion can produce a variety of energy products:

![Captured in a ground level macro perspective are natural resources showcasing sustainable biomass energy possibilities through decomposition. Dried organic layers interspersed with pebbles and darker detritus suggest the raw material foundation for renewable energy generation. The image highlights energy conservation potential and the eco-friendly aspect of biomass as a carbon footprint reducer, aligned with climate change mitigation strategies.](https://energy.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/sustainable-biomass-energy-potential-in-decomposing-natural-resources-for-eco.jpg "Sustainable Biomass Energy Potential in Decomposing Natural Resources for Eco Friendly Solutions")

### The Big Picture

The concept of Algal Biomass Conversion is rooted in the desire for renewable and sustainable energy sources. Algae’s ability to capture CO2 during growth makes it a potentially carbon-neutral energy source. By converting algal biomass, we can create fuels and energy while mitigating the effects of [climate Meaning → The Climate domain addresses the science, impacts, and policy solutions related to global climate change and adaptation.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/)  change.

**Challenges Ahead** → While Algal Biomass Conversion holds promise, several challenges remain. These include reducing the cost of algal cultivation, optimizing conversion processes for efficiency, and scaling up production to meet energy demands.

Algal Biomass Conversion offers a promising pathway to renewable energy by transforming algae into usable fuels and energy products.

**Future Directions** → Research and development efforts are ongoing to address these challenges and further develop Algal Biomass Conversion technologies. Innovations in algal strains, cultivation techniques, and conversion processes are continually being explored. With continued progress, Algal Biomass Conversion could play a significant role in a sustainable energy future.

![This digital abstract image visualizes a futuristic sustainable energy network, employing metallic and dark textures to represent complex infrastructure. The artwork embodies concepts of resource management and energy efficiency within a circular economy model. The interconnected geometric forms suggest a smart grid design, optimized for clean power generation and minimal environmental impact.](https://energy.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/sustainable-energy-network-abstract-visualization-for-resource-management-and.jpg "Sustainable Energy Network Abstract Visualization for Resource Management and Green Technology")

## Intermediate

Building on the fundamental understanding of Algal Biomass Conversion, this section explores the process in greater detail, focusing on specific methods, technological hurdles, and economic considerations. We will examine the various stages involved, from algal cultivation to final energy product, and discuss the trade-offs inherent in different conversion pathways. The definition of Algal Biomass Conversion extends beyond a simple transformation to encompass a complex interplay of biological, chemical, and engineering principles aimed at maximizing energy yield and minimizing environmental impact.

![The image presents an abstract view of a sophisticated, angular space based solar power station, embodying sustainable energy principles. Its composition is characterized by repeating geometric forms and detailed solar panels, suggesting advanced technological sustainability. Against a backdrop of deep blue, possibly space, the structure stands as a symbol of clean energy generation and efficient energy design.](https://energy.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/innovative-space-based-solar-power-station-showcasing-sustainable-energy.jpg "Innovative Space Based Solar Power Station Showcasing Sustainable Energy Technology and Efficient Resource Management")

### Algal Cultivation → The Foundation

The cultivation of algae is a crucial first step that significantly impacts the overall economics and [sustainability Meaning → Sustainability is the capacity for human civilization to coexist with the Earth's biosphere in a state of equilibrium.](https://sustainability-directory.com/)  of Algal Biomass Conversion. Two primary cultivation systems are commonly employed:

The scene emphasizes resource efficiency and ecological balance in energy transition.](https://energy.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/abstract-sustainable-infrastructure-for-energy-transition-on-arid-planet.jpg "Abstract Sustainable Infrastructure for Energy Transition on Arid Planet Emphasizing Planetary Resilience")

### Biochemical Conversion Pathways → Harnessing Biology

Biochemical conversion pathways utilize biological processes to break down algal biomass and convert it into valuable products.

![This striking abstract image embodies the essence of energy efficiency and sustainable resource management within a geometric framework. Sharp lines and contrasting colors highlight the crucial intersection of modern design and environmental responsibility. The composition evokes concepts of renewable energy integration and clean energy infrastructure, pointing towards a future where buildings and structures minimize their carbon footprint.](https://energy.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/abstract-geometric-energy-efficiency-design-emphasizing-sustainable-resource.jpg "Abstract Geometric Energy Efficiency Design Emphasizing Sustainable Resource Management and Conservation")

### Thermochemical Conversion Pathways → Applying Heat

Thermochemical conversion pathways involve the use of heat to transform algal biomass into energy products.

**Lipid Extraction** → A specific focus is lipid extraction for biodiesel production. Algae, especially certain strains, can accumulate significant amounts of lipids (oils), which can be extracted using solvents or mechanical pressing. The extracted lipids are then transesterified to produce biodiesel, a fuel that can be used in diesel engines. Solvent extraction is the most common method, using solvents such as hexane to dissolve the lipids.

Mechanical pressing, while less efficient, avoids the use of solvents. Supercritical fluid extraction using CO2 is another promising technique.

![Two juxtaposed transparent spheres take center stage, visually representing the layered facets of sustainable energy and resource management. The image's focus on light refraction embodies the dynamic nature of energy reflection and transmission, core principles in green energy systems. Set against a backdrop transitioning from deep blue to warm brown, the artwork implies a shift towards sustainable infrastructure and eco-conscious energy consumption.](https://energy.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/abstract-spherical-forms-symbolizing-transparent-sustainable-energy-solutions.jpg "Abstract Spherical Forms Symbolizing Transparent Sustainable Energy Solutions and Reflective Resource Management Practices")

### Challenges and Opportunities

Algal Biomass Conversion faces a number of technological and economic challenges:

![Imagine a self-sustaining marine structure elegantly floating on the vast ocean, a powerful visual representation of sustainable architecture and energy independence. This futuristic construction, intricately comprised of cream and blue marble geometric forms, showcases resilient infrastructure thoughtfully designed for a demanding marine environment. The artistic composition effectively symbolizes clean energy innovation, potentially leveraging consistent ocean energy as a dependable renewable energy source addressing off-grid energy needs.](https://energy.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/innovative-floating-island-architecture-promotes-marine-energy-independence-and.jpg "Innovative Floating Island Architecture Promotes Marine Energy Independence and Sustainable Resilient Urbanism")

### Economic Considerations

The economic viability of Algal Biomass Conversion depends on a variety of factors, including algal biomass yield, conversion efficiency, energy prices, and government incentives. Detailed economic analyses are needed to assess the profitability of different Algal Biomass Conversion pathways. The significance of government support and subsidies in the initial stages of development cannot be overstated.

The explanation behind the [future Meaning → Exploring future scenarios and possibilities is the core focus of the Prism sustainability satellite.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/)  scope includes the exploration of integrated biorefineries, where algal biomass is used to produce a range of products, including fuels, chemicals, and animal feed, can improve the economics of Algal Biomass Conversion. Carbon capture and utilization (CCU) technologies, where CO2 from industrial sources is used to grow algae, can further enhance the sustainability of Algal Biomass Conversion. The economic landscape shifts with technological advances and evolving market demands.

Effective algal cultivation, efficient conversion methods, and strategies to tackle economic hurdles will be key to widespread adoption of Algal Biomass Conversion.

**Moving Forward** → Overcoming these challenges requires ongoing research and development efforts, as well as supportive government policies and private sector investment. With continued progress, Algal Biomass Conversion has the potential to become a significant contributor to a sustainable energy future. The intention is clear → create a reliable and environmentally sound alternative to fossil fuels.

## Academic

Algal Biomass Conversion, from an academic perspective, represents a complex, multidisciplinary field situated at the intersection of biotechnology, chemical engineering, and environmental science. At its core, the academic definition recognizes Algal Biomass Conversion as the orchestrated series of biological, chemical, and physical processes designed to transform algal-derived organic matter into a spectrum of bio-based fuels, chemicals, materials, and energy vectors. The academic meaning ascribed to this field extends beyond mere energy production, encompassing the broader implications for sustainable resource management, carbon sequestration, and the development of circular bioeconomies. This necessitates a rigorous, systematic approach to research, development, and deployment, underscored by life cycle assessment, techno-economic analysis, and adherence to the principles of green chemistry.

![This image showcases precision engineered industrial components designed for sustainable technology and energy efficiency. The contrast between the textured, aged wooden block and the modern blue cylindrical casing emphasizes the integration of diverse materials in sustainable design for energy systems. Central to the composition is a golden metallic component suggesting critical functionality within a power generation or energy conversion system, underscoring resource optimization and material sustainability.](https://energy.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/sustainable-engineering-precision-components-for-industrial-energy-efficiency.jpg "Sustainable Engineering Precision Components for Industrial Energy Efficiency and Material Longevity")

### A Critical Re-Evaluation of Algal Biomass Conversion

Following rigorous scholarly analysis, including diverse perspectives, encompassing socio-economic considerations, and multi-cultural influences, this academic endeavor redefines Algal Biomass Conversion as → “A strategically orchestrated, technologically intensive, and ecologically conscientious paradigm shift aimed at leveraging the inherent photosynthetic capabilities of diverse algal species to synthesize and sequester atmospheric carbon dioxide into a portfolio of value-added bioproducts, thereby contributing substantively to climate change mitigation, resource diversification, and the establishment of resilient, distributed bioeconomy frameworks capable of addressing global energy, food, and material demands.” The long-term consequences of this statement require careful evaluation. Focusing specifically on the implication of global energy demand, a deeper analysis of Algal Biomass Conversion’s potential to alleviate this pressure is warranted.

The escalating global energy demand, projected to increase substantially in the coming decades, necessitates the exploration of sustainable and renewable alternatives to fossil fuels. ### Challenges to Widespread Implementation

Despite these advantages, several challenges impede the widespread implementation of Algal Biomass Conversion for addressing global energy demands:

### Overcoming the Obstacles

Addressing these challenges necessitates a multifaceted approach:

**Policy and Investment** → Implementing supportive government policies and attracting private sector investment to accelerate the development and deployment of Algal Biomass Conversion technologies. Government initiatives like the US Department of Energy’s Bioenergy Technologies Office are central to these advances. Incentivizing investment through tax breaks, grants, and loan guarantees can encourage innovation and commercialization.

### A Global Perspective

The delineation of the future involves fostering international collaborations. Algal Biomass Conversion can contribute to achieving the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), particularly those related to energy, climate action, and responsible [consumption Meaning → Consumption refers to the use of goods and services, with a sustainable focus on mindful, responsible, and circular patterns.](https://eco.sustainability-directory.com/)  and production. International collaborations and knowledge sharing are critical for accelerating progress in this field. The establishment of international standards and best practices can also promote sustainable and responsible development.

### The Road Ahead

The realization of Algal Biomass Conversion’s full potential requires a concerted effort from researchers, engineers, policymakers, and industry stakeholders. The path towards a sustainable energy future hinges on continued innovation, collaboration, and a commitment to environmental stewardship. The global energy demand can potentially be alleviated by prioritizing and continuing innovation and research of Algal Biomass Conversion.

The key to unlocking Algal Biomass Conversion’s potential lies in continuous innovation, focused research, strategic policies, and global collaboration, ultimately leading to a greener, more sustainable energy future.

# Conversion of algal biomass into renewable fuel: A mini review of chemical and biochemical processes

![Zahidul Islam Rony](https://loop.frontiersin.org/images/profile/1892699/74)
![M. Mofijur,
](https://loop.frontiersin.org/images/profile/616836/74)
![M. M. Hasan](https://loop.frontiersin.org/images/profile/2150924/74)
![Shams Forruque Ahmed](https://loop.frontiersin.org/images/profile/1424516/74)
![Irfan Anjum Badruddin](https://loop.frontiersin.org/images/profile/1327722/74)
![T. M. Yunus Khan](https://loop.frontiersin.org/cdn/images/profile/default_32.jpg)

Microalgae are a vital resource for the coming years to address the concern of decrease in oil reserves and the negative impacts of fossil fuels on the environment. Their utilization is crucial for a wide range of industrial applications. Depending on the strain, microalgae contain a variety of chemical components and can be treated biochemically or thermochemically. This review thus focuses on the biochemical mechanisms that are used to convert algal biomass into sustainable fuel, including the challenges and potential of those processes. Microalgae have been shown to be a viable third-generation alternative to conventional biofuel feedstocks. The optimum production of biofuel depends on the proper selection of microalgae species based on their lipid, carbohydrate, and protein content in order to produce high-quality, sustainable biofuel. *Nannochloropsis gaditana* can contribute to a maximum biodiesel yield of 96.47%, whereas *Nannochloropsis oculata* can produce the least (25%) through the biochemical process of transesterification. Higher yields of microalgae-derived gaseous, solid and liquid fuels can be achieved by pre-treating microalgal biomass and then employing bioconversion processes such as photo-fermentation and hydrothermal carbonization.

## 1 Introduction

The pressing demand for energy, the diminishing sources of fossil fuels, and the mounting environmental concerns all call for a promising alternative fuel including biofuel (biodiesel, biomethane, bioethanol etc.) ([Mofijur et al., 2013a](#B67); [Chuah et al., 2016](#B20); [Chuah et al., 2017](#B19)). Microalgae, as a feedstock for biofuels have demonstrated a significant amount of potential. This is as a result of the fact that it matures with copious mass creation in a matter of days as opposed to terrestrial crops and plants, which can take anything from a few months to many years to reach this stage of development ([Wang et al., 2007](#B99); [Yu et al., 2017](#B104); [Chia et al., 2018](#B18)). The primary benefits of using biofuels from the microalgae are that they do not require the use of potable water, they can be grown on land that is not suitable for agriculture, they require only simple nutrients like CO2 and sunlight, they do not require the use of herbicides or pesticides during cultivation, they can use carbon-neutral feedstock, and they can produce high-quality pyrolytic materials ([Singh et al., 2011](#B90); [Lee and Ofori-Boateng, 2013](#B49); [Debiagi et al., 2017](#B21); [Chia et al., 2018](#B18); [Gan et al., 2018](#B31)). In addition, the high photosynthetic efficiency of microalgae, which ranges from 6% to 8%, when compared to the photosynthetic efficiency of terrestrial biomass, which ranges from 1.8% to 2.2%, as well as the high CO2 fixation by microalgae, makes them excellent biofuel feedstocks and natural environment remediators ([Kumar et al., 2017](#B47); [Yu et al., 2017](#B104); [Yu et al., 2018](#B103); [Brigljević et al., 2019](#B11)). In addition to this, it has been observed that the bulk of microalgae is composed of more than 70% fat when measured based on their dry weight ([Liang et al., 2010](#B53)). Because of their high lipid content, microalgae are an important component in the manufacture of biofuels. Cultivating microalgae can, over the course of time, result in both the production of economically viable feedstock and the decrease of greenhouse gases.

The cultivation of microalgae for commercial purposes takes place in a particular sort of bioreactor that creates ideal growth conditions, resulting in high levels of output. At this time, open raceway ponds and closed photobioreactors are both utilised for the production of microalgae, and the selection of one or the other is determined by the particular requirements. Raceway ponds that are open to the environment are frequently used for the treatment of wastewater, whereas closed photobioreactors are typically utilised for the production of pharmaceutical and value-added goods. The quantity of biofuel produced and the quality of that biofuel are both highly dependent on a number of parameters, including the properties of the microalgae, the conditions of the production process, the catalyst type and dosage, and the configuration of the reactor ([Azizi et al., 2018](#B6)). The effect of these elements should be thoroughly investigated and modified in order to get the best output and quality of biofuels from microalgae. This will allow to reach goals. Few researchers already studied different microalgae conversion processes ([Rowbotham et al., 2012](#B80); [Marcilla et al., 2013](#B61); [Chen et al., 2015a](#B16); [Elliott, 2016](#B28)). A large number of macromolecular components serve as substrate for the biochemical conversion process, which is one of these processes. As a result, the benefit of using microalgae in the biochemical conversion process is that it results in a reduced level of process complexity while simultaneously providing an energy form together with a mineralized digestate that is rich in beneficial nutrients. Thus, the purpose of this mini review is to highlight the utilisation of different biochemical processes and the challenges and future prospects of those processes to convert algal biomass into sustainable fuel.

## 2 Conversion technologies of microalgae into biofuels

Converting microalgae into biofuels can be accomplished through the use of a variety of different technologies. As shown in [Figure 1](#F1), these conversion methods can be categorised into two primary categories: biochemical and thermochemical. Because certain biomass feedstocks include high levels of lipids, it is possible to extract those lipids using either physical or chemical separation methods. After that, the triglyceride portion of the extracted lipids can be converted into biodiesel by using the transesterification process. The biochemical conversion processes also result in the production of biogas or ethanol, and the conversion itself is a somewhat slow process, typically lasting hours, days, or weeks depending on the kind of feedstock ([Bridgwater, 2010](#B10)). Thermochemical conversion including pyrolysis and gasification process, on the other hand, results in the production of several products in a relatively short amount of time and the quality of the products that are intended for consumption may frequently be improved with the application of catalysts.

![www.frontiersin.org](https://www.frontiersin.org/files/Articles/1124302/fenrg-11-1124302-HTML-r1/image_m/fenrg-11-1124302-g001.jpg)

**FIGURE 1**. Various conversion technologies for biofuel production, modified from ([Suali and Sarbatly, 2012](#B95)).

One of the most important steps in ensuring that the production of biofuel is both economically viable and environmentally sustainable is making the choice of the appropriate conversion technology. At this point in time, there are no obvious advantages that can be demonstrated between the thermochemical and biochemical routes. However, according to a review that was done by Sims and colleagues ([Sims et al., 2010](#B89)), thermochemical conversion is regarded as being more favourable. The properties of the feedstock are one of the most significant challenges for biochemical conversion. Feedstock modifications, such as those involving pre-treatment processes, call for a significant investment of capital, which, in turn, drives up production costs. In addition, the efficiency with which biomass may be converted into biofuel by using biochemical processes is quite low. In addition, the biochemical pathway could only result in the production of a single type of end product for each application, in contrast to the thermochemical pathway, which may result in the production of multiple types of end products from a single operation. In the next subsections, a concise discussion of each kind of conversion technology is presented.

### 2.1 Chemical and biochemical conversion technologies of microalgae

#### 2.1.1 Transesterification of microalgae

The typical process that is utilised to convert the triglyceride found in microalgal lipid into biodiesel is called transesterification ([Sinha et al., 2008](#B91); [Rajan and Senthilkumar, 2009](#B76)). This is the reaction that occurs when TG is combined with an acyl-acceptor ([Fan and Burton, 2009](#B29); [Helwani et al., 2009](#B34)). Acyl-acceptors might come in the form of carboxylic acids, alcohols, or even another ester. When alcohol is used as the acyl-acceptor in transesterification, glycerol is produced; however, triacylglycerol is produced when ester is utilised ([Ma and Hanna, 1999](#B57); [Dubé et al., 2007](#B26); [Ranganathan et al., 2008](#B77); [Sharma et al., 2008](#B83); [Robles-Medina et al., 2009](#B79)). According to the principles of stoichiometry, 3 mol of alcohol are needed for every mole of TG. In most cases, however, a greater molar ratio of alcohol is employed in order to accomplish the highest possible level of biodiesel generation. This molar ratio changes depending on the type of feedstock that is used, the type of catalyst that is used, the temperature, and the amount of time that the reaction takes. Methanol, ethanol, and propanol are the three types of alcohol that are utilised most frequently ([Mofijur et al., 2013b](#B68); [Mofijur et al., 2014](#B69); [Rozina et al., 2022](#B81)). In point of fact, the yield of biodiesel is not affected by the type of alcohol that is used; rather, the selection of alcohol is determined by cost ([Al-Zuhair et al., 2007](#B4); [Sinha et al., 2008](#B91)).

The process of transesterification that makes use of a catalyst is referred to as a catalytic transesterification process, whereas the process that does not make use of a catalyst is referred to as a non-catalytic transesterification process ([Gerpen, 2005](#B32); [Meher et al., 2006](#B65); [Marchetti et al., 2007](#B60)). In addition, a catalytic process might be one of two categories: homogeneous or heterogenous, and this classification is determined by the catalyst that is being employed ([Fjerbaek et al., 2009](#B30); [Lin et al., 2009](#B54)). It has been reported that the heating value of biodiesel made from microalgae is 41 MJ/kg ([Xu et al., 2006](#B101)) and this value is in accordance with the ASTM D6751 standards ([Li et al., 2007](#B52)). According to a number of studies, the enzyme that comes from lipase is fantastic for the process of converting a variety of vegetable oils to methyl ester. The transesterification process in generally take place at 35–50°C at atmospheric pressure and a molar ratio of 3:1–6:1 ([Fjerbaek et al., 2009](#B30); [Mata et al., 2010](#B62)).

#### 2.1.2 Fermentation

The production of ethanol from organic substrates like sucrose, bagasse, cellulose, or starch is the goal of the metabolic process known as fermentation ([McKendry, 2002a](#B64); [Lee and Lee, 2016](#B50)). The fermentation of starch, sugar, and cellulose found in microalgal biomass is the primary route that leads to the synthesis of ethanol from microalgae ([Kim et al., 2011](#B45)), with starch making up the majority of the carbohydrate content and having a potential range of up to 60 dw% depending on the culture conditions ([Nahak et al., 2011](#B70); ([Brányiková et al., 2011](#B9)). Controlling the amount of nitrogen or iron present during culture of microalgae can result in increased starch production ([Liu et al., 2008](#B55); [Dragone et al., 2011](#B25)). In its most basic form, fermentation can be broken down into two categories: ([Chuah et al., 2016](#B20)) aerobic and ([Chuah et al., 2017](#B19)) anaerobic, with the distinction between the two based on whether or not the process requires oxygen.

Before microalgae biomass or starch can be fermented, it must first undergo pretreatment in the form of milling, which turns the biomass or starch into sugars. Next, the sugars are combined with *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* yeast and water in a fermenter for the fermentation process ([Demirbaş, 2001](#B23)). After fermentation, the diluted alcohol that was generated is put into a holding tank where it waits to be distilled. The ethanol concentration in the diluted alcohol product typically ranges from 10% to 15% ([McKendry, 2002a](#B64)). The product can be purified through the distillation process, which also results in the production of concentrated ethanol that has a purity level of 95% and is therefore appropriate for use as a supplement or substitute for transport fuel ([McKendry, 2002b](#B63)). This fermentation process uses less energy because the CO2 produced can be used to grow microalgae and the leftover material can be recycled as a medium for microalgae growth or used as animal feed ([McKendry, 2002a](#B64); [Milledge et al., 2014](#B66)).

There are four primary processes that take place during the fermentation of ethanol. Ethanol has replaced gasoline in many vehicles over the last decade because to its ability to reduce emissions of CO2 and many harmful pollutants (CO, NO) during combustion ([Demirbas, 2008](#B22)). As a transportation fuel, bioethanol is widely available on the market and is utilised often ([Walker, 2010](#B98)). Nevertheless, bioethanol has a few downsides, including the fact that it is caustic, has a low energy value, is hazardous to the ecology, and has a low vapour pressure. However, in order to achieve long-term benefits and sustainability, additional efforts are required to upgrade bioethanol to a higher quality fuel that can stand alone and is compatible with fossil fuel. At the moment, bioethanol is ready to be integrated with the fuel that is already being used for transportation. *C. vulgaris* is a type of microalgae that is excellent for the production of bioethanol. This species has a high starch content (about 37%), which enables it to achieve up to 65% of ethanol conversion ([Adeniyi et al., 2018](#B2)). In addition to Chlamydomonas, Dunaliella, Scenedesmus, and Spirulina, which all contain more than fifty percent starch, there are also species that are thought to be promising candidates for the synthesis of bioethanol ([Hu et al., 2012](#B37)). [Table 1](#T1) provides a summary of the findings of studies conducted on the production of biofuel using various biochemical processes.

![www.frontiersin.org](https://www.frontiersin.org/files/Articles/1124302/fenrg-11-1124302-HTML-r1/image_m/fenrg-11-1124302-t001.jpg)

**TABLE 1**. Summary of the findings of studies conducted on the production of biofuel from microalgae using various biochemical processes.

### 2.2 Anaerobic digestion of microalgae

When the biomass to be converted has a high-water content, the biochemical process of anaerobic digestion becomes crucial. In this process, biogas, NH3, and CO2 are produced, along with other undesirable gases like H2S from the biomass’s organic content ([Mahmudul et al., 2021](#B58)). Microalgae biomass, both pre-treated and untreated, and lipid extraction leftovers can be employed to generate methane ([Siddiki et al., 2021](#B88)). Steps of hydrolysis, fermentation, and methanogenesis follow one another in the digestive process. First, the organic matter is broken down into soluble sugars, and then the bacteria ferment those sugars to produce methane and carbon dioxide. As a result of the digesting process, a gas is produced that contains 20%–40% of the lower heating value of the feed in terms of energy. To get the best possible end result from the anaerobic digestion process, it is necessary to tune the hydraulic retention, organic loading parameters, pH, and temperature ([Ahmed et al., 2021](#B3)). The majority of current biogas research is devoted to improving wastewater treatment and finding ways to integrate biogas generation with other algal-based manufacturing techniques. In addition to lowering odours, hazardous gas emissions, and oxygen demand in wastewater, anaerobic digestion of microalgae has a few other advantages.

## 3 Challenges of chemical/biochemical conversion of microalgae

Capital-intensive efforts to convert algae into biofuels have been advocated for on the basis of the plant’s rapid expansion, manageable size, and high oil content. There are a number of challenges that need to be addressed before algae can emerge as a financially feasible substrate to replace petroleum and, thus, decrease CO2 release. These challenges range from where and how to cultivate these algae to advancements in oil extraction and fuel processing ([Hannon et al., 2010](#B33)). Some of the challenges of converting microalgae into biofuel through chemical/biochemical conversion processes ([Siddiki et al., 2022](#B87)) are discussed as follows.

• The process of conversion is greatly governed by environmental factors.

• The products made through biochemical processes are more selective and can be processed at lower temperatures.

• Ideally suitable for processing algal biomass with a moisture content higher than 50%.

• The first step in this process of transformation is the pre-treatment of the feedstock.

• The slower reaction rate necessitates longer residence times for product formation.

• Biological conversion is a major bottleneck that reduces productivity. More money would need to be spent on infrastructure upgrades, like a larger reactor, to accommodate the increased production.

• To achieve any kind of feasible findings, the use of bacteria, enzymes, or chemicals is essential. Sludge, an undesirable waste product, is produced and needs to be treated in some way.

• Due to the biological nature of the conversion process, the amount of production is relatively low.

• In most cases, limited to a few products and additional microorganisms are required to produce additional products.

## 4 Future prospects of chemical/biochemical conversion of microalgae

There are challenges, but at the current rate of development, biofuels derived from microalgae may be capable of competing with existing energy sources within decades. Industrial-scale chemical/biochemical conversion of microalgae into biofuels will require concerted efforts to optimise strains and increase production volumes. As a result, on the basis of the most recent state of the art, the following are some proposals for potential future developments.

• The productivity of a specific strain of microalgae can be increased to some degree by updating the design of the photobioreactor, regulating the physical conditions, giving growth nutrients, and adding metal ions. Further progress can be made by improving strains utilising recombinant DNA technology, which could lead to enhanced metabolic pathways that concentrate more biofuel-grade compounds.

• In order to make the process of algal harvesting more efficient, it is possible to make use of cutting-edge methods like fungi-mediated harvesting and the application of tailored nanoparticles. This will make it easier to separate the farmed microalgae from the broth in which they were grown.

• The amount of biofuel that can be extracted from harvested microalgae depends on the pre-treatment processes that are employed. Additionally, the linking of more than one pre-treatment processes has emerged as a promising strategy; nevertheless, this technique requires additional study before it can be used at industrial scales.

• The application of physicochemical and physical pre-treatments in combination have shown the enhancement of carbohydrate release from algal biomass.

• The improvement of efficiency with which enzymes transform sugar into its constituent molecules.

• The enhancement of fermentation efficiency through nutritional supplementation of both liquid and gaseous media.

## 5 Environmental viability assessment of microalgal biofuels

Microalgae are able to withstand and absorb significantly higher quantities of CO2 than terrestrial plants. Because of this, microalgae are able to utilise CO2 released from petroleum-based power stations or other industrial sources, which can reduce the production of glasshouse gas ([Nigam and Singh, 2011](#B72)). It is possible to transform the entire algal biomass as well as extracts of algal oil into various forms of fuel. Because algae are able to extract nutrients like nitrogen and phosphorus from a wide range of wastewater sources, they are able to provide a sustained bioremediation of these wastewater sources, which has benefits for both the environment and the economy ([Shilton et al., 2008](#B84)). Carbon credits can be generated through the use of algal biofuels thanks to their ability to connect the generation of CO2-free fuel with the sequestration of CO2 from other power industries ([Dismukes et al., 2008](#B24)). When compared to terrestrial feedstock crops, algae have a number of advantages that make them more desirable. Despite the fact that their development requirements are comparable to those of terrestrial plants, they make very effective use of the resources available to them and as a result, they have great productivity despite using a relatively small amount of water ([Brown and Zeiler, 1993](#B12); [Chelf et al., 1993](#B14)). The algae take in the additional CO2 that is present, storing it as biomass as a result of their accelerated rate of development. Microalgae offer other benefits in addition to their potential for capturing CO2 from fossil fuel power plants. This is because direct CO2 capture procedures are preferred to indirect CO2 capture processes. Passing flue gases directly through a media where microalgae can grow is an efficient technique to capture the CO2 in such streams ([Benemann, 1997](#B7)). While it’s likely to be extremely expensive to apply CO2 directly to terrestrial crops via enclosures, an indirect technique that uses flue gases to stimulate land species could be cost-effective while being much less direct and less efficient ([Packer, 2009](#B73)).

## 6 Conclusion

Increased interest in microalgae biofuel production has been driven by rising concerns about the use of fossil fuels, greenhouse emissions, and the conflict between food and fuel when it comes to conventional biofuel sources. This study reviewed the biochemical mechanisms used to convert algal biomass into sustainable fuel, as well as its challenges and potential. Microalgae were found to be the largest renewable, clean energy resource with the potential to replace fossil fuels. Cultured microalgae in a photobioreactor could meet the biomass requirements for continuous biofuel generation in addition to repairing CO2 emissions and treating wastewater. The best method for converting wet biomass into biofuel is the biochemical conversion process provided that the biomass’s moisture content is higher than 50%. Although microalgae hold great potential, neither upstream nor downstream methods now available can completely tap into this potential. Consecutive growth of microalgae requires extremely efficient and cost-effective processing in order to provide sustainable and high-quality biofuel production and reduce reliance on fossil fuels. However, there is still a significant knowledge gap in microalgal biofuel research since it is difficult to compare the chemical composition of different feedstocks to that of synthesized products. To bridge the gaps for future development, more comprehensive characterisation studies are needed.

SUMMARY:
Thermochemical conversion of microalgae includes pyrolysis, gasification, liquefaction and direct combustion . In pyrolysis, microalgae biomass is thermally degraded in the absence of oxygen. Pyrolysis of microalgae biomass is capable of producing medium to low calorific value biofuels in a large-scale facility.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Assessing various methods like biochemical and thermochemical processes for converting algal biomass into energy, focusing on efficiency and yield optimization.
Conducting comprehensive cost-benefit analyses of algal-derived fuels and products, including production costs, market trends, and potential government incentives.
Analyzing the ecological effects of algal systems on CO2 capture, water quality improvement, and overall sustainability, ensuring minimal negative impacts on ecosystems.
